listings.title: Men Of Mark In The World Of Sport In NZ Vintage 1906 Illustrated Biographies
listings.body:
Men Of Mark In The World Of Sport In New Zealand publication stands as an early twentieth century biographical historical record focusing heavily on the foundational figures of horse racing, breeding, and colonial athletic codes. This substantial volume, printed by The Brett Printing and Publishing Company on Shortland Street in Auckland, compiles numerous short profiles detailing colonial sporting personalities, administrators, jockeys, owners, and turf enthusiasts active across the colony during the late nineteenth and opening years of the twentieth centuries. Factual contents and layout features include approximately two hundred and fifty individual illustrations comprising photographic portrait plates of prominent figures alongside historical depictions of sporting activities. Included among the structural records are biographical entries for significant identities such as regional turf club officials, racing club presidents, livestock auctioneers, and stable masters. Documentation includes historical insights into early metropolitan and provincial racing organizations such as the Avondale Jockey Club, alongside regional developments in breeding operations like the Cambria Park Stud Company. The entries outline career achievements, administrative milestones, and contributions to local athletics, rugby, yachting, and equine disciplines. This text incorporates contemporary commercial print listings from early colonial suppliers including gunmakers, saddlers, totalisator manufacturers, and regional transportation enterprises like Hatrick and Company Wanganui River Tourist Steamer Line.

listings.title: The Road to War Great South Road 1862-64 1863 Redoubts South Auckland History
listings.body:
This historical monograph documents military operations and infrastructure development within the South Auckland and Waikato sectors. Coverage spans the construction of the Great South Road to the Waikato River and the subsequent establishment of military redoubts and defensive installations. Detailed records focus on the escalating armed conflicts between Pakeha and Maori forces, tracking troop movements from the opening stages of the campaign through to the major engagement at the Battle of Rangiriri. Specific focus is directed toward the logistics of frontier defense lines and tactical river movements.

The provided index and chapter structure details specific military locations, engagements, and units:
• The Making of the Road and movements down the road to the front
• The frontier line including Wairoa Redoubts, Whangamarino, and Meremere
• The Thames Expedition covering Miranda, Esk, and Surrey redoubts
• The engagement at Rangiriri and the death of Captain Mercer
• Appendices recording blockhouses, redoubts, and historical mileposts
• Documented historical accounts of Pukekohe East Church and Havelock
• Operational roles of the Royal Artillery in the Maori War and site surveys of existing redoubts

Documentation relies on verified archival material, field surveys conducted up to 1980, and chronological records of the 1862-1864 period. The bookincludes specific geographic data, troop allocation breakdowns, and engineering specifications for northern frontier defensive fortifications.

listings.title: The Road to Sarau - Upper Moutere  History German Migration  Nelson Area
listings.body:
Germany early 1800s role New Zealand Company North German Mission Society
Voyage St Pauli Colonial Nelson German settlers Moutere Valley St Paulidorf
Floods drought German settlement Waimea river mouth arrival Skiold prosperity

This historical volume documents the migration and settlement patterns of German immigrants in the Nelson and Tasman regions during the nineteenth century. The text details the initial conditions in Germany during the early 1800s, analyzing the structural role of the New Zealand Company in organizing and managing early colonial expeditions. Specific focus is directed toward the North German Mission Society and its operational frameworks, featuring comprehensive missionary profiles of key figures including Riemenschneider, Wohlers, Heine, and Trost. 

Maritime migration routes are recorded through documentation of the voyage of the St Pauli and the subsequent arrival of the Skiold. The geographical distribution of these European arrivals is traced through regional developments in Colonial Nelson, the establishment of the mission section within the Moutere Valley, and the formation of St Paulidorf. Environmental challenges affecting agricultural viability and sustainability in the district are documented, focusing on the cyclical patterns of severe floods and prolonged drought periods that challenged the community. 

Further chapters evaluate secondary regional locations, detailing the German settlement at the Waimea river mouth and the broader struggle for economic prosperity following the abandonment or transition of St Paulidorf.

listings.title: Definitive Regional History Of Levin 385 pages
listings.body:
Levin The Making of a Town is a definitive regional history written by prominent New Zealand historian Anthony Dreaver. Published in 2006 by the Horowhenua District Council, this comprehensive 385-page volume was commissioned to mark the centennial jubilee of Levin achieving borough status in 1906.

Rather than standard local histories that focus strictly on timelines, Dreaver’s work sets the birth and maturation of Levin against the wider economic, political, and social dynamics of the lower North Island.
Historians praise Levin: The Making of a Town for its deep dedication to detail and factual administrative accuracy. Rather than relying on a generalized chronicle, Dreaver utilizes comprehensive archival logs, population statistics, local council debates, and a definitive biographical index of mayors, councillors, and civic leaders to illustrate exactly how a modern New Zealand rural service town is built from the ground up.
* Forward chronology and population statistics of levin
* Dune wetland and forest environmental history records
* Three visions of levin and early town emergence phases
* Making a community development and new borough status infrastructure
* Belief and action social movements and out of town development logs

* The great sewerage debate and local public health infrastructure logs
* Riding the current and a sound of hammering industrial development files

* Appendix listing past mayors councillors and official council officers
* Comprehensive historical reference notes bibliography and index logs

The book compiles extensive primary data tracking early pre-colonial maritime entries, custom logs, and port registries to establish an accurate chronology of regional contact. Statistical summary tables categorize all recorded shipping visits by their initial port of registry and specific trade classification, mapping the commercial evolution of the northern anchorage. Substantial research focus details the structural growth and operational patterns of the early whaling industry, tracking both shore-based and deep-sea vessels. The main core of the volume presents a comprehensive chronological list of all verified vessels entering the harbor over the thirty-seven year period. For genealogical and research cross-referencing, the volume includes extensive separate data systems consisting of a dedicated ship index and a comprehensive individual name index.

* Introductory historical overview analyzing early maritime contact and data collection sources.
* Summary tables organizing all recorded shipping entries by international port of registry.

* Dedicated historical tracking mapping the development and peak growth of the whaling industry.
* Comprehensive chronological registry listing all vessel arrivals and departures from 1803 to 1840.
* Integrated ship index detailing technical names and tracking markers for individual vessels.

Included among the structured records are accounts of early dairy farming operations, butter factory establishments, and pioneer family lineages who settled the valley. Documentation focuses on the immense physical challenges presented by the native bush, the wet West Coast climate, and the rugged, bush-clad hills that kept the settlement isolated for decades. The chapters outline the development of local infrastructure, detailing how the community operated when roads were entirely metalled and Highway 12 remained unsealed. This volume stands as a crucial reference text for genealogical researchers, Northland regional historians, and individuals tracking colonial farming expansion, timber industry history, and early pioneer life within the Hokianga district.

This historical booklet compiles regional sports records, team milestones, and administration details from the club based in the Rangitikei district. Factual contents and layout features include 50th Jubilee Celebrations itinerary for the 3rd, 4th, and 5th of September 1982, official club crest insignia, and comprehensive historical timelines of local rugby developments. Documentation spans early club formations prior to World War One disruptions, subsequent revivals during the interwar period, and post World War Two reorganizations through to the 1982 jubilee year. Included are structural listings of club captains, committee members, match statistics, regional representative players, team achievements, photographic captions, local sports grounds records, and foundational club patron details. This publication serves as a key reference for sports history research within the Marton and broader Rangitikei provincial rugby circles. It incorporates archival summaries, season by season progressions, senior and junior grade team records, internal trophy awards, match summaries, local competition charts, player rosters, and historical anecdotes detailing the development of the club over its fifty active years of registration within the regional union framework.

listings.title: Hasketts in New Zealand Irish Emigration Settlement History - Jenny Hodges
listings.body:
Regional and family history detailing the migration of the Haskett family from Ireland to New Zealand during the mid-to-late nineteenth century. Compiled by Jenny Hodges, this softcover publication focuses on genealogical records, shipping logs, and local settlement details connecting the family from County Tipperary to Canterbury.

Content and Origins
The text documents the journey of various Haskett family members originating from the market town and civil parish of Borrisokane (Buiríos Uí Chéin) within County Tipperary, Ireland. It tracks their arrival via early assisted immigration schemes into Lyttelton, Canterbury. Key historical voyages recorded in the text include:

Chrysolite (27 July 1861): Carrying Phoebe Eliza Haskett (aged 18, domestic servant), Robert Haskett (26), Esther/Hester Haskett (24), Louisa Haskett (20), and Thomas Haskett (22).

Zealandia (23 May 1862): Carrying Eliza Hayes Haskett (19) and John Haskett (22).

Glenmark (14 November 1867): Carrying Joseph Haskett and his wife Dora.

Zealandia (9 December 1871): Carrying Henry Haskett (18) and Annie Haskett (16).

The publication examines family connections, distinguishing between confirmed siblings and more distant regional cousins sharing the Borrisokane origin. It contextualises their arrival against the early development of Christchurch and the Canterbury province, noting the proximity of their arrival dates to the establishment of the region in 1850.

listings.title: Seeker of Souls and Gold - Golden Bay Gold Hunting Pioneer William Barker SIGNED
listings.body:
This biographical volume details the historical timeline, religious ministries, and mining expeditions of an early nineteenth-century immigrant across the Taranaki and Nelson districts. The text traces his origins in Yorkshire, documenting his decision to leave his home and family to sail in 1842. The records follow his initial deployment as a pioneer preacher dedicated to Christian mission work within the Pacific, charting his interactions, language acquisition, and established affinity with the Maori people during the early colonial era.

Physical hardships and regional environmental conditions are documented, specifically focusing on the damp climate and severe privations of bush life in South Taranaki. These factors resulted in chronic health complications that forced a relocation to the healthier sunshine of Nelson. The text records his subsequent domestic utility among the white settlers in the northern South Island, where he maintained active Methodist preaching circuits while transitioning into geographic exploration and active gold hunting crusades throughout the wider Tasman region.

The narrative details his strenuous endeavours to balance spiritual crusading with physical prospecting, providing insights into the economic and social conditions of early mining settlements up until 1885. The text incorporates local geographic markers, family tracking data, and historical references concerning the growth of early religious institutions and regional gold fields.

listings.title: History of the Foxton and Wanganui Railway
listings.body:
History of the Foxton and Wanganui Railway that evolved from a wooden tramway in 1873 for traffic between Foxton and Palmerston North.

It was the first publicly owned passenger-carrying line in the North Island of New Zealand, for it predated the first railway out of both Auckland and Wellington. In 1876 the old wooden tramway was converted to an iron railway and extended northwards ultimately to join another railway being built southwards from Wanganui.

listings.title: Sowers of Seed Anglican Church History Waiheke Island Hauraki Gulf Parish
listings.body:
This historical volume outlines the establishment, growth, and regional administration of the Anglican Church across Waiheke Island, incorporating supplementary records regarding missionary outreach on Great Barrier Island within the wider Hauraki Gulf. The text compiles early ecclesiastical documentation to trace how the local parish developed from isolated pioneer ministries into structured municipal congregations. The records focus heavily on the early layout of coastal settlements such as Oneroa, Ostend, and surrounding bays, tracking how land procurement and church construction were managed by early regional residents and visiting clergy under the oversight of the Anglican Diocese of Auckland

The documentation tracks the spiritual and logistical challenges faced by isolated island communities, examining the transport limitations, financial dependencies, and communication lines connecting the outer gulf islands to the central mainland diocese. Specific analytical focus is directed toward the parish records, detailing baptism registers, marriage logs, building fund contributions, and the socio-religious networks introduced by nineteenth and twentieth-century church leaders

Further sections provide insights into the shared pastoral care systems that bridged the remote settlements of Great Barrier Island with the developing institutional frameworks of the Waiheke parish. The compilation includes monochrome historical photographs, layout maps of local church sites, and transcribed correspondence from early congregation members, serving as a primary genealogical and ecclesiastical reference for tracking family names and institutional developments within the northern regional ministry

listings.title: Hawkes Bay Provincial Centennial 1858-1958 Regional Anniversary Booklet
listings.body:
This historical commemorative publication documents the regional development, pastoral growth, and civic history of the Hawkes Bay province during its first century of official settlement spanning 1858 to 1958. The text outlines the initial separation from the Wellington province, detailing the legislative and administrative steps required to establish independent local government. Analytical focus is directed toward early infrastructure projects, tracking the surveying and establishment of major municipal centers including Napier, Hastings, Wairoa, Waipukurau, Waipawa, Dannevirke, and Woodville, while charting the expansion of transport networks across the wider district boundaries.

Agricultural and pastoral industries serve as a primary focus, with comprehensive records on the clearing of the backcountry and the establishment of major sheep stations that underpinned the regional economy. The text covers the evolution of farming techniques, local wool production markets, and the introduction of refrigeration which transformed the export capabilities of the East Coast. Detailed timelines trace consecutive administrative eras, tracking how coastal shipping networks transitioned into formal rail links and public roadways connecting the isolated rural communities to central urban ports.

Social institutions, early educational facilities, and municipal works are systematically recorded alongside descriptions of regional geographic markers and land deployment patterns. The publication compiles historical data regarding the pioneer families and civic administrators who shaped the district layout, serving as a primary regional reference for tracking land titles, local municipal transitions, and economic milestones achieved over the hundred-year jubilee period.

listings.title: TRADITIONAL ART  Maori Carving Illustrated Cultural History Design Principles
listings.body:
Maori Carving Illustrated

Maori Carving Illustrated provides a detailed and structured introduction to the methods, history, and spiritual underpinnings of traditional whakairo.

The text holds significant cultural, anthropological, and historical value, detailing the profound spiritual influence of tapu on traditional craftsmen and the creative process. It thoroughly analyzes key iconographical elements—including the marakihau, the one-eyed figure, the three-fingered hand, complex spirals, the lizard, and the ngutuihe—explaining how these features integrate into functional and ceremonial objects. From the intricate adornments on weapons, tools, and waka huia to the large-scale architectural achievements of carved meeting houses and canoes, the book traces the historical evolution of carving from early forms through to distinct regional variations such as Arawa and East Coast styles.

Originally published in 1955 by A.H. & A.W. Reed, this edition 1972

listings.title: RURAL HISTORY | Packtrack To Highway Kumara Gold Rush Feilding Kimbolton History
listings.body:
Packtrack to Highway provides a warm, honest, and firsthand chronological account of a pioneering style of life on the early frontiers. This volume serves as a vital historical and social record for the West Coast and Wellington regions, documenting the immense physical hardships, high-stakes gold mining operations, and raw determination required to carve out a living along the remote back-country tracks and diggings of the nineteenth century.

The narrative holds significant historical, local, and genealogical value, capturing the lived experiences of a gold miner's son who witnessed the historic 1879 gold rush to Kumara. The text methodically chronicles the atmospheric reality of early mining settlements—complete with cosmopolitan crowds, lively dance halls, and streets illuminated by kerosene lanterns on poles. From early childhood memories of sluicing claims to adolescent years spent digging tunnel tail races alone through the night, the author paints a vivid picture of the goldfields, including the thrill of uncovering substantial nuggets for early employers. 

Beyond the West Coast diggings, this publication follows the author's adventures as a young man exploring the northern frontier settlements of the Wellington province. It preserves detailed descriptions of working alongside surveying parties, packing stores down dense forest tracks, driving coaching teams between Feilding and Kimbolton, hunting wild pigs near Karioi, and mustering wild horses on the tussock plains south of Ruapehu. It stands as an invaluable reference for regional historians and researchers tracking the social development, early surveying expeditions, and rugged transport networks of early New Zealand.

Ex library worn And repaired Dustjacket - Great read  136 pages

listings.body:
Early Proposals Construction Begins Wartime Complications List of Tunnels
Final Stages of Construction Railway Completed Summary of Opening Dates
Gradient Profile Fifty Years of Train Service List of Stations Map Reference

This publication records the engineering, development, and operational history of the regional rail link connecting the Taranaki district with the main trunk line at Okahukura in the King Country. The documentation begins with early proposals put forward during the initial planning phases, tracking the political and geographical debates surrounding the route selection. Legislative approval and surveying led into the next phase as construction begins, detailing the manual labor, machinery, and surveying methods used to clear the rugged terrain of the backcountry.

Progress was significantly altered by external geopolitical factors, documented in the sections detailing wartime complications, which caused material shortages and labor scarcity. The heavy engineering demands of the route are systematically cataloged, including a comprehensive list of tunnels that required extensive boring through challenging geological formations.
Chronological milestones are preserved within a summary of opening dates for specific sections of the line, supplemented by a detailed gradient profile showing the engineering elevations and track angles required to traverse the regional ridges. The final chapters examine fifty years of train service, documenting the steam and diesel locomotives that operated across the network, changes in freight demands, and a complete list of stations detailing the regular stops, halts, and junctions that serviced local communities along the line.

listings.title: Legacy Journal Vol 19 No 3 2007 Tom Walsh North Shore Auckland Photography
listings.body:
LEGACY VOLUME 19 NUMBER 3 2007 - JOURNAL OF THE FEDERATION OF HISTORICAL SOCIETIES


Contents:
* Tom Walsh: North Shore historian, journalist and political activist
* Fairs & Steel: their impact on Auckland photography
* A lonely cairn
* The first historical society?
* Book reviews

This publication provides factual local history data, pioneer biographical accounts, and early professional trade records for the Auckland and North Shore districts. It documents the political activism, journalism careers, and local research contributions of regional historian Tom Walsh. It also details the commercial and technical impact of practitioners Fairs and Steel on the development of early portraiture and documentary photography in the Auckland area. Additional research papers explore early monument structures and track the origins of regional heritage movements. The volume serves as a reference source for regional history researchers, genealogists, and tracking historic photographic trades.

- Condition VG - Date 2007 - Pages 20

listings.title: Legacy Journal of Federation of Historical Societies Volume 18 Number 1 2006
listings.body:
LEGACY VOLUME 18 NUMBER 1 2006 - JOURNAL OF THE FEDERATION OF HISTORICAL SOCIETIES

This book contains regional historical articles, heritage preservation updates, and research papers compiled by the Federation of Historical Societies in 2006. The publication features multiple studies on heritage preservation, local histories, and historic maritime vessels.

Contents:
* 50 years of Caring for our Heritage
* A Doctor’s Round
* Birds of a Feather
* Letters of a Patient Lover
* Gallery
* Glory Restored
* More on the ‘Ralph Bernal’
* History in Print
* Federation History Award Guidelines
* Classified Advertisements

This national publication provides factual heritage data, institutional preservation updates, local biographical details, and print review indexes for regional historians. It documents community efforts in landmark preservation, maritime histories related to the vessel Ralph Bernal, and established criteria for regional historical awards. The volume serves as an informative reference source for local history groups, genealogists, and heritage researchers tracking historic sites, regional publications, and structural restoration projects.
